title: Kazakhstan in Antiquity: Human Evolution and the Formation of Nomadic Civilization
overview: Lecture 1 covers the deep prehistory of Kazakhstan from the appearance of the first hominids about 2.5 million years ago to the emergence of the first nomadic states (Saka, Usun, Kangly, Huns) in the 1st millennium BCE. It introduces the main theories of human origin, the periodization of the Stone Age (Paleolithic → Mesolithic → Neolithic → Eneolithic), the Bronze Age Andronovo culture, the domestication of the horse in the Botai culture, and the early statehood of the Eurasian steppe nomads who became the ethnocultural ancestors of the Kazakhs.

coreConcepts.definition: The evolutionary process by which humans emerged from the animal kingdom; the framework for biological theories of human origins.
coreConcepts.example: Classified under genus Homo, species Sapiens.

coreConcepts.definition: Yuval Noah Harari's concept: a leap in brain capacity that gave Homo sapiens language, abstract thought, complex social structures and dominance over the planet.
coreConcepts.example: Use of fire, large brain, tool use, learning capacity, complex social organization.

coreConcepts.definition: Earliest Lower Paleolithic stone-knapping technique using struck flakes from quartzite and flint cores.
coreConcepts.example: Borykazgan, Tanirkazgan, Kemer sites in southern Kazakhstan.

coreConcepts.definition: Small, finely worked stone blades inserted into bone or wooden hafts to make composite tools (arrows, harpoons, sickles).
coreConcepts.example: Hallmark of the Mesolithic (10,000–5,000 BCE) on the Irtysh, Ishim, Tobol and Ural river basins.

coreConcepts.definition: Transition from a foraging economy to settled agriculture and animal husbandry; introduction of polished stone tools, pottery, weaving and early metallurgy.
coreConcepts.example: Mahandzhar (Central KZ), Atbasar (Northern KZ), Kelteminar (Western KZ) cultures.

coreConcepts.definition: Eneolithic culture of Northern Kazakhstan (3rd–2nd millennia BCE) credited with one of the world's earliest horse domestications.
coreConcepts.example: Pottery fragments at Botai contain residues of fermented mare's milk (koumiss).

coreConcepts.definition: Bronze Age cultural complex (2nd millennium BCE) covering Kazakhstan, southern Siberia, the Urals and Central Asia. Sedentary-pastoral economy, metallurgy, chariots, kurgan burials in stone-slab 'boxes'.
Iron Age Iranian-speaking nomads (9th–3rd c. BCE) – the ethnocultural successors of Andronovo. Named 'Scythians' in Greek sources, 'Tura' in Avestan, 'Saka' in Persian sources.
coreConcepts.example: Behistun Inscription of Darius I (~520 BCE) mentions Saka north of the Syr Darya.

coreConcepts.definition: Distinctive Saka decorative art (7th–6th c. BCE) using stylized predators, deer and mythic creatures on weapons, harness fittings and jewelry.
coreConcepts.example: Found on the Issyk Golden Man's costume and on Berel horse trappings.

expandedNotes.heading: Theories of Human Origin
expandedNotes.body: The manual lists six approaches: (1) Religious – divine creation; (2) Darwin's Evolutionary Theory – descent from great apes through mutation and natural selection, basis for the concept of anthropogenesis; (3) Linear Model – single descent line: Australopithecus → Pithecanthropus → Paleoanthropus (Neanderthal) → Cro-Magnon; (4) Multiple Coexisting Species – at least six Homo species (Australopithecus, Neanderthalensis, Soloensis, Erectus, Denisova, Sapiens) lived simultaneously until the 8th millennium BCE; (5) Displacement – Homo sapiens out-competed and replaced other species; (6) Inter-species Crossbreeding – assimilation of other Homo species into the sapiens line. Harari's Cognitive Revolution rests on five factors: large brain, tool use, high learning capacity, complex social organization, control of fire (which altered diet and physiology).

expandedNotes.heading: The Stone Age in Kazakhstan
expandedNotes.body: The Stone Age spans roughly 2.5 million – 3,000 BCE and is divided into Paleolithic, Mesolithic and Neolithic. PALEOLITHIC stages: Early/Lower (2.5 million – 300,000 BCE) – pebble tools; Middle (300,000 – 40,000 BCE) – flake & blade retouching; Late (40,000 – 10,000 BCE) – microliths and specialized tools. Homo erectus inhabited the Karatau region of southern Kazakhstan by ~1 million years ago. Notable Lower Paleolithic sites: Borykazgan, Tanirkazgan, Kemer (south). Middle Paleolithic: Obalysay, Kudaikol, Zhaman-Aibat (Central), Kolgutti River (East), Semizbuga, Shakpak-Ata (Central), Arysandy & Sarysu rivers (South/Central), Mangistau (West). Late Paleolithic: Ashysai (South), Kanay, Peshchera (East). Over 130 large petroglyph clusters of this era exist.

expandedNotes.heading: Mesolithic (10,000–5,000 BCE)
expandedNotes.body: Transitional period after the end of the last Ice Age (~12,000 years ago). Climate became warm and humid; mammoths and woolly rhinos went extinct; deer, wild horses and boars proliferated. Mesolithic sites cluster along the Irtysh, Ishim, Tobol and Ural rivers; the Kyzylsu site on Mangystau holds many advanced stone tools. Key innovations: invention of the bow and arrow, microliths used as arrow and harpoon tips, bone harpoons and fishhooks, scrapers and chisels. Dwellings were lightweight wooden frames covered with hides – portable, suited for seasonal camps near water.

expandedNotes.heading: Neolithic Revolution (5,000–3,000 BCE)
expandedNotes.body: A 'qualitative leap': grinding, drilling and sawing of polished stone tools; first agriculture (wheat, barley) and animal husbandry (sheep, goats). Invention of fire-resistant clay – the first artificial material – enabled pottery decorated with geometric patterns. Major archaeological cultures: Mahandzhar (Central KZ, Mahandzhar and Duzbay sites in the Turgai region), Atbasar (Northern KZ), Kelteminar (Western KZ, also into Karakalpakistan). Other notable sites: Kyzylsu (East), Teskiktas (Central), Kulsary, Shebir, Senek (West, Mangystau), Karaungur (South). Society grew complex with tribal unions and the beginnings of social stratification (farmers, artisans, warriors).

expandedNotes.heading: Eneolithic and the Botai Culture
expandedNotes.body: The Eneolithic ('Copper Age', 3rd millennium BCE) introduced metal alongside stone. In the southern zone, copper-tool use spread with agro-pastoral economies; in the steppe zone, stone-working actually intensified. The Botai culture in Northern Kazakhstan (Botai, Krasny Yar, Tersek sites) is famous for the early domestication of the horse – confirmed by changes in horse teeth/joints and by traces of mare's-milk lipids in Botai pottery. Horse domestication revolutionized mobility, trade, and warfare and enabled the rise of nomadic pastoralism.

expandedNotes.heading: Bronze Age and the Andronovo Culture
expandedNotes.body: Bronze (copper + tin) was invented in the Eurasian steppes around the turn of the 2nd millennium BCE. The Andronovo culture, named after a Siberian village near the first excavations, spread over southern Siberia, Kazakhstan, the Urals and Central Asia. Settlements lay along riverbanks; the economy combined pastoralism (with horse herds as the most prized asset) and irrigation agriculture. Burial custom: bodies placed crouched on their side inside stone-slab 'boxes' under kurgan mounds; occasionally cremation. Pottery shifted from round-bottomed to flat-bottomed with rich geometric ornament. Metalwork included spiral bracelets, gold leaf earrings, headdresses. Three stages: Nurinsk (early, Nura River), Atasu (middle, Atasu River), Begazy-Dandybai (late, near Karaganda / North Balkhash). The proto-urban settlements of Kent (Central KZ) and Arkaim (Northern KZ) had fortifications, water-supply systems, sanctuaries and craft quarters. Tamgaly petroglyphs (Almaty region) – 5,000 images of epic heroes, sun-faced beings, chariots and ritual dances – were inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List in 2004.

expandedNotes.heading: The Saka (9th–3rd centuries BCE)
expandedNotes.body: Saka were Iranian-speaking nomads of the Early Iron Age, ethnocultural successors of Andronovo. Persian sources name them 'Saka', the Avesta calls them 'Tura with swift horses' (origin of 'Turan'). Greek and Roman authors (Herodotus's Histories, Polybius, Pliny, Ptolemy, Strabo) called them 'Scythians' and listed sub-groups: Massagetae (between the Syr Darya and Amu Darya), Sauromatae/Sarmatae (West KZ), Issedones, Arimaspi. Persian sources distinguish: Saka para-darya (trans-river Saka), Saka tigrakhauda (pointed-hat Saka on the lower Syr Darya and Amu Darya), Saka haumavarga (haoma-drink-preparing Saka around Balkhash), Arimaspi (guardians of the griffins' gold in the Altai and East KZ).

expandedNotes.heading: Saka Society, Religion and Wars
expandedNotes.body: Society was hierarchical: military aristocracy (decided on war and peace, formed alliances), priests (religious rituals), commoners (shepherds and farmers). 'Kings' held political power; the military aristocracy ensured a form of steppe democracy in which each Saka was a free and equal member. Religion was an early form of Zoroastrianism without temples or clergy – worship under open sky, on hilltops, by household fires or rivers. Wars: Queen Zarina fought the Median Empire for Parthia; Queen Tomyris of the Massagetae defeated and killed Persian King Cyrus the Great in 530 BCE; Darius I subjugated some Saka groups and listed them on the Behistun Inscription; Saka cavalry fought alongside the Persians at Marathon (490 BCE) in the Greco-Persian Wars; later Saka beyond the Syr Darya halted Alexander the Great's eastward expansion.

expandedNotes.heading: Saka Archaeology and the Golden Man
expandedNotes.body: Major Saka burial complexes: Besshatyr Mound on the right bank of the Ili River (31 kurgans); Issyk burial complex 50 km east of Almaty (40+ kurgans) where in 1969 archaeologist Kemal Akishev uncovered the 'Golden Man' – a young warrior in gold-embroidered garments and a conical hat with gold plates depicting animals; the burial also contained a silver bowl with a runic-like inscription. The Berel Mounds in East Kazakhstan (~70 kurgans) yielded a royal burial with horses in full regalia, excavated by Z. Samashev in 1998. Elements of Saka material culture – long trousers, short kaftans, felt hats (ancestor of the Kazakh 'kalpak') – influence Kazakh dress to this day.

expandedNotes.heading: The Huns (3rd c. BCE – 5th c. AD)
expandedNotes.body: The Huns (Xiongnu) were a Turkic-speaking nomadic confederation that emerged on the territory of modern Mongolia in the 3rd century BCE. Chinese chronicles ('Shiji' by Sima Qian, 'Hanshu' = Book of Han) and Greco-Roman authors (Ammianus Marcellinus, Priscus) are the main written sources. The supreme ruler bore the title shanyu/chanyu. Society had a strict hierarchy: shanyu → military aristocracy & clan nobility → free commoners (herders, artisans) → slaves and serfs. Modu Chanyu (209–174 BCE) united the tribes of Central Asia, forced Han China to recognize the Huns as equals and pay tribute, and controlled key trade oases. His successor Laoshang (174–161 BCE) defeated the Wusun and Donghu. In the 2nd century BCE constant raids led China to build the Great Wall. In the 1st century BCE the empire split into Northern and Southern Huns; Zhi Zhi Chanyu (50–36 BCE) moved west and established a fortified center in the Talas Valley (modern KZ). From the late 4th century AD the Huns crossed the Volga, defeated the Ostrogoths and Visigoths, settled in Pannonia (Hungary), and under Attila (434–453) built an empire from the Volga to the Rhine. After Attila's death (453) and the defeat at Nedao (454), the empire disintegrated. Huns are credited with inventing the polychrome jewelry style (gold/silver inlaid with garnet, amethyst, turquoise, carnelian, glass paste and enamel).

expandedNotes.heading: Usuns and Kangly (3rd c. BCE – 4th c. AD)
expandedNotes.body: Usuns occupied Zhetysu (Semirechye) between the Ili and Chu rivers and parts of Eastern Kazakhstan and Northern Kyrgyzstan. State formed in the 2nd c. BCE. Ruler title: kunbi. They allied diplomatically and militarily with Han China against the Huns, including dynastic marriages. Fortified capital Chiguchen (Kungut in modern terms). Famous artifact: the 'Kargaly Diadem' (1939, Almaty region) – ~500 g of gold inlaid with turquoise, carnelian and lapis lazuli, made with filigree and granulation, probably for a priestess or noble. Kangly state: 3rd c. BCE – 4th c. AD, in the Syr Darya valley extending to Turkestan and Zhetysu; also called 'Kangju' in Chinese sources. Mixed economy: pastoralism, irrigation agriculture in oases, crafts (metalworking, ceramics, jewelry). Key settlements: Bityan, Kokmardan. Both peoples practiced shamanism, nature-spirit cults, ancestor worship and animal sacrifice.

keyScholars.name: Charles Darwin
keyScholars.contribution: Theory of evolution by natural selection — basis of the modern theory of anthropogenesis.

keyScholars.name: Yuval Noah Harari
keyScholars.contribution: Author of Sapiens; framed the Cognitive Revolution that made Homo sapiens dominant.

keyScholars.name: Queen Tomyris
keyScholars.contribution: Ruler of the Massagetae; defeated and killed Persian King Cyrus the Great in 530 BCE.

keyScholars.name: Queen Zarina
keyScholars.contribution: Saka leader who fought the Median Empire for control over Parthia.

keyScholars.name: Darius I
keyScholars.contribution: Persian king; Behistun Inscription (~520 BCE) names Saka tribes living north of the Syr Darya.

keyScholars.name: Herodotus
keyScholars.contribution: Greek 'Father of History'; described Eurasian Scythians/Saka in his Histories (5th c. BCE).

keyScholars.name: Sima Qian
keyScholars.contribution: Chinese historian; the 'Shiji' is the principal written source on the Huns, Usun and Kangju.

keyScholars.name: Ammianus Marcellinus
keyScholars.contribution: Roman historian whose 'Res Gestae' gives the earliest detailed Latin description of Hun lifestyle and military tactics.

keyScholars.name: Modu Chanyu
keyScholars.contribution: First great Hun shanyu; unified Central Asian tribes and forced tribute from Han China.

keyScholars.name: Attila
keyScholars.contribution: Built the Hun Empire from the Volga to the Rhine; invaded Gaul and Italy.

keyScholars.name: Kemal Akishev
keyScholars.contribution: Kazakh archaeologist; discovered the Issyk Golden Man in 1969.

keyScholars.name: Zainolla Samashev
keyScholars.contribution: Archaeologist; in 1998 led the excavation of the royal Saka burial at Berel in East Kazakhstan.

comparativeTables.title: Stone Age Periodization
rows: Flakes, blades, retouched scrapers, knives, points
Polished axes, adzes, ceramics, first metallurgy

comparativeTables.title: Andronovo Culture: Three Stages
rows: Dandybai village near Karaganda; Begazy / North Balkhash

comparativeTables.title: Saka Groups in Written Sources
rows: Between Syr Darya and Amu Darya

realWorldExamples: The Issyk Golden Man (1969) is the official symbol of the modern Kazakh state and appears on coins, monuments and the Independence Monument in Almaty.
Tamgaly petroglyphs (Almaty region) joined the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2004 with about 5,000 images.
The Botai culture's domestication of the horse is now widely cited as one of the world's earliest, predating European examples.
The traditional Kazakh felt hat 'kalpak' descends directly from Saka headwear.
Steppe democracy of the Saka – every free man considered equal under a chosen 'king' – is invoked in modern Kazakh national narratives of statehood.

timeline.event: Beginning of the Paleolithic; Homo habilis in East Africa
Homo erectus inhabits the Karatau region (southern KZ)
Late Paleolithic – Homo sapiens fully developed; first art and burial rites
End of Ice Age; transition to Mesolithic
Neolithic Revolution – farming, pottery, polished tools
Queen Tomyris defeats and kills Cyrus the Great
Behistun Inscription of Darius I names the Saka
Saka cavalry fights at the Battle of Marathon alongside Persia
Saka halt Alexander the Great's advance east of the Syr Darya
Issyk Kurgan – burial of the 'Golden Man' (discovered 1969 by K. Akishev)
Modu Chanyu unifies the Huns; tribute treaty with Han China
Formation of the Usun state in Zhetysu; Kangju in the Syr Darya valley
Zhi Zhi Chanyu founds a fortified Hun centre in the Talas Valley
Reign of Attila – peak of Hun power
Death of Attila; the Hun Empire disintegrates after Nedao (454)

mustMemorize: Paleolithic begins ~2.5 mln BCE and is divided into Early, Middle, Late
Andronovo culture = Bronze Age of Kazakhstan (2nd millennium BCE); three stages: Nurinsk → Atasu → Begazy-Dandybai
Botai culture = world's earliest evidence of horse domestication (3rd mill. BCE)
Saka tribes (Persian sources): tigrakhauda, haumavarga, para-darya; Greek sources call them Scythians
Tomyris defeated Cyrus the Great in 530 BCE
Issyk 'Golden Man' discovered in 1969 by K.A. Akishev
Modu Chanyu (209–174 BCE) – first great Hun ruler; Attila (434–453) – peak of Hun Empire
Usun ruler title – kunbi; Usun capital – Chiguchen (Kungut); famous artifact – Kargaly Diadem
Kangly/Kangju state in the Syr Darya valley, 3rd c. BCE – 4th c. AD
Tamgaly petroglyphs inscribed on UNESCO World Heritage List in 2004
cheatSheet:
Paleolithic → Mesolithic → Neolithic → Eneolithic (Botai) → Bronze Age (Andronovo) → Iron Age (Saka)
Saka kings: Tomyris (vs Cyrus 530 BCE) | Zarina | Issyk Golden Man (Akishev, 1969)
Huns: Modu (209 BCE) → Laoshang → Zhi Zhi (Talas) → Attila (434–453)
Usun – Zhetysu, kunbi, Kargaly Diadem | Kangju – Syr Darya, Bityan
mnemonics: P-M-N-E-B – Paleolithic, Mesolithic, Neolithic, Eneolithic, Bronze (the 5 prehistoric epochs in order)
T-H-P – Saka groups Tigrakhauda, Haumavarga, Para-darya
S-U-K-H – Saka, Usun, Kangly, Huns (ethnocultural sequence of nomads)
'530 = Tomyris paid the price' (Cyrus paid with his life that year)
